title: Collecting a swarm into a hive
subtitle: Bringing a hanging swarm from branch to box.
excerpt: A swarm hanging in a bush or on a low branch can be hived in 20 minutes with a box, a veil, and some patience. This covers the standard bump-and-tip method for accessible swarms, plus what to do when the swarm is in an awkward spot.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), swarm collection guidance. BBKA Swarm leaflet (current). Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), chapter on swarms, Internet Archive.

content.text: clustered in a garden hedge or on a low branch is not a crisis. The bees have no hive to defend and are gorged with honey, which makes them unusually calm. A prime swarm collected within the first few hours before scouts have agreed on a cavity tends to go into a box without difficulty. The challenge is collecting quickly enough: a
that has been hanging for more than 24 hours may already be moving to a new site.

Take a nuc box or hive body with frames, a veil, gloves, a saw or secateurs, and a sheet of cardboard or a cotton sheet to cover the box opening temporarily. If the swarm has been in place for more than a day, light the smoker too. Leave it unlit otherwise: smoke can disperse a fresh swarm rather than calming it.

Place the box directly under the cluster. For a swarm on a low branch, hold the box just below the mass and give the branch one sharp, firm shake. The cluster drops into the box as a single unit. Cover the top immediately and set the box down on the ground with the entrance pointing toward the original cluster site.

The queen is almost certainly in the main ball of bees. If she went into the box with the first shake, the flying bees outside will fan at the entrance within a few minutes, then walk in. If you watch the bees clustering on the outside and streaming downward into the box, the queen is already inside. If the bees outside are moving upward and regrouping into a ball, the queen did not make it in: repeat the shake.

When the swarm is on a branch too high to box directly, cut the branch and lower it carefully rather than shaking from below. A long-handled box or a stepladder may be needed. If the cluster is on a flat surface (a fence post, a wall), brush the bees gently into the box with a large soft brush or a piece of card.

containing a virgin queen is harder to hive than a prime swarm. Casts are smaller (sometimes just a few thousand bees) and the virgin queen is skittish and runs rather than clustering tightly. If the bees keep leaving the box before the entrance is established, it is usually a cast with a queen that has not yet decided to stay.

Close the entrance with a plug of foam or grass and transport the box in the evening if possible, when all flying bees have returned. Place the hive or nuc on a permanent stand at least 5 m from any other hive. Open the entrance fully after dark.

Check after 10 days for eggs. A successfully hived prime swarm in April or May should be laying within a week of collection; the queen was mated before she left and starts laying as soon as she has drawn comb to work with. If the colony is in a nuc and building well by week three, transfer to a full hive body before the nuc becomes congested.

attrs.intro: Common problems after hiving a swarm.

items.fix: Wait until evening, check whether any bees remain, re-collect if the cluster has reformed elsewhere.
items.cause: The queen was not collected, or this is a cast with an unsettled virgin queen.
items.symptom: Bees leaving the box within hours of hiving

items.fix: Wait until day 21 before intervening. If still no brood at day 21, introduce a new queen or a frame of eggs from another colony.
items.cause: If it was a cast, the virgin queen may still be on a mating flight. If a prime swarm, the queen may have died.
